explanation_md: Objects are constructed in declaration order and destroyed in reverse order. `b` and `c` are created inside the inner scope: `b` first, then `c`. When the inner scope closes, `c` is destroyed first (reverse order), then `b`. Finally `a` is destroyed at the end of `main`.

prompt_md: A class allocates heap memory in its constructor. When is its destructor guaranteed to run for a stack-allocated object of that class?

options.a: Only when the programmer explicitly calls `delete` on the object
options.b: At the end of the program, regardless of scope
options.c: When the object goes out of scope (the enclosing block ends or the function returns)
options.d: The destructor never runs automatically; the programmer must call it manually

explanation_md: For a stack-allocated object, the destructor runs automatically when the object's scope ends — when the enclosing block closes or the function returns. This is the key guarantee of RAII. `delete` is only needed for heap-allocated objects created with `new`.

options.a: `data` should be initialised to `nullptr` before `new` is called
options.b: `Buffer` has no destructor, so `data` is never freed — memory leak on every object
options.c: The constructor should use the initialiser list instead of assignment
options.d: `b` should be allocated with `new Buffer{4}` to avoid a stack overflow

explanation_md: Because `Buffer` has no destructor, the heap array allocated in the constructor is never freed when `b` goes out of scope. Every `Buffer` object leaks its array. The fix is to add `~Buffer() { delete[] data; }`.

prompt_md: What is the danger of explicitly calling a destructor on a stack object, like `myObj.~MyClass();`?

options.a: It is a compile error — destructors cannot be called explicitly
options.b: The destructor runs once manually and then runs again automatically when the object goes out of scope, causing a double-destroy
options.c: The object's memory is freed immediately, freeing the stack frame prematurely
options.d: Nothing dangerous — calling the destructor manually is equivalent to `delete`

explanation_md: Calling a destructor manually on a stack object is legal syntax, but then C++ will also call it automatically when the object goes out of scope. If the destructor frees heap memory (e.g. `delete[] data`), the second automatic call is a double-delete — undefined behavior.
The constructor prints `A` when `new R` is executed. Then `B` is printed. `delete p` calls the destructor (prints `Z`) and then frees the memory. Finally `C` is printed.
